title: harden: audit fixes across codec, proxy, transport, config-write, and eval
body:
Module-by-module audit of the codebase surfaced a set of real defects. This PR fixes them, most-load-bearing first. Full suite green (567 passed), ruff + mypy clean, rebased on latest `main` (incl. #89/#90).

## Losslessness & measurement credibility (the core claims)
- **Verify-before-emit for the always-on Tier-0/0.5 codec.** The cross-call *diff* tier self-checks inline (`diff_encode` only returns a delta that decodes back exactly), but tabularize/dictionary shipped through `policy.apply` **without** the same gate — a latent codec bug could reach the model silently, defeating the lossless-first premise. `apply` now re-decodes the emitted text, and on any mismatch falls back to minified-lossless with a warning. This was the highest-leverage finding: the newest tier was safer than the foundational one.
- **measure** zeroes banked savings when the round-trip gate fails, so a downstream aggregator that forgot to filter on `roundtrip_ok` can't inflate the headline % with a payload the proxy would never emit.
- **fluency/scoring** `_matches_number` no longer counts a value embedded in an identifier/version (`record_6`, `v6.2`) — a narrow tightening that leaves the validated fluency baseline unmoved.
- **fluency/pack + report** score each form by its own trial count instead of a shared `max` that understated sparser forms.

## Config-corruption & security
- **`_secure_io.write_restricted` is now atomic** (temp + `fsync` + `os.replace`), fixing the non-atomic `~/.claude.json` / `policy.json` writes at the primitive — a crash mid-write can no longer truncate the user's live config. Symlink refusal preserved; no orphan temp/empty file on failure.
- **transport SSRF**: refuse link-local/cloud-metadata targets (`169.254.169.254`, `metadata.google.internal`, `fe80::/10`) — the downstream URL can come from an untrusted repo-committed `.mcp.json`. Loopback/LAN MCP servers still allowed.
- **transport**: cap downstream response body at 128 MiB (was an unbounded `resp.read()`).
- **multiproxy**: bound the per-peer send queue (was unbounded; a stalled HTTP peer grew memory without limit). Drops for the broken peer instead; `close()` can't deadlock on a full queue.
- **cli**: `policy generate` warns before overwriting hand-authored `capture:false` privacy rules.

## Robustness & observability
- **proxy** exit-drain 2s → 30s with a truncation warning (2s could cut a large final reply on child exit).
- **proxy** side-effect sink (capture/audit/stats) failures announced once unconditionally, not only under `--debug`, so a silently-dead ledger is noticed.
- **tokenize** warns once when tiktoken is present but broken (was masked as an indistinguishable silent `None`).
- **capture.load_corpus** surfaces the count of skipped corrupt envelopes.
- **history** uses restricted perms + a rotation cap.
- **install_mcp.default_repo_path** gets a 10s timeout on the git subprocess.
- **lossy** drop handle is wider and injection-proof (unambiguous JSON-array hash input, 64-bit) + warns when a truncate-marked field is a non-truncatable type.

## Tests
+9 regression tests: verify-before-emit fallback, atomic-write preserves the prior target on failure, link-local SSRF block (+ loopback/LAN still allowed), and measure gate-fail zeroing.

## Deliberately deferred (documented, not silently skipped)
`_fold_records` key-order drift (value-lossless; a fix would kill the tabularization win), the stats-ledger rotation TOCTOU (self-heals, benign), and the `probes` cross-server pairing (already shelved per #64). These carry less risk than the change to fix them would add.

title: install-mcp: classify_server_sensitivity (install-time never-lossy suggestion)
body: First slice of increment 2 (install-time classifier). Pure helper flagging whether a server looks credential/personal (name + command vs `policy.SENSITIVE_SERVER_RE`) so `install-mcp` can prompt to bake it into `never_lossy_servers`. Suggestion only — operator adds innocuous-named stores (kb, sb-run) by hand; the runtime floor (PR #89) enforces the pattern independently. Wiring into `do_install`'s interactive flow is the next slice. Verified: new test passes, ruff + mypy clean, 558 tests green.

title: policy: server-level lossy exclusion (never-lossy servers forced lossless)
body:
## What

First increment of lossy productization — the **mandatory safety floor** the security review required, landed *before* any lossy transform turns on.

On a **never-lossy server** (credential/personal store), `truncate`/`drop-to-retrieve` are structurally forbidden — `apply()` forces 100% lossless even if a rule marks a field lossy. Enforced on the **verified server identity** (`#83`), not a tool-name match, so a mislabeled/renamed rule can't leak a credential payload through a lossy transform.

**Two layers** (both, per the security expert):
- `Policy.never_lossy_servers` — a per-server list, to be **baked at `install-mcp` time** by the sensitivity classifier (next increment). Catches sensitive servers *by identity*, including ones whose names look innocuous (a personal KB, a launcher alias like `sb-run`).
- `SENSITIVE_SERVER_RE` — a **non-overridable name floor** (`secret|credential|vault|token|password|key|auth`) a policy typo cannot defeat.

Mirrors the existing `Rule.capture` guard: a declarative security property, safe by construction rather than by operator discipline.

## Why now

Lossy is still dark (no policy enables it). This lands the floor first so the moment a field is marked lossy, credential/personal servers are already fenced off — not one policy edit away from a leak.

## Verification

- 3 new tests: baked-list suppression, non-overridable floor, ordinary-server lossy still applies, predicate + JSON-load coverage
- `apply()` on a never-lossy server → decompress == original (fully lossless) + a clear warning
- **557 tests pass**; ruff + mypy clean

## Next increments (not this PR)

1. **Install-time classifier** — `install-mcp` identifies sensitivity (name/command pattern + optional `tools/list` probe), prompts to confirm, bakes `never_lossy_servers`.
2. **Trust-aware suggestion signal** — stop `policy generate` recommending load-bearing fields (the step-1 finding: it greedily suggested dropping `principle`).
3. `terse tune` one-command UX; docs.

title: release: tag-based versioning (hatch-vcs) + GitHub Release CI
body:
## What

Second slice of the smart-install workstream: **the git tag becomes the single source of version truth**, and a tag push cuts a GitHub Release. Builds on #87.

- **`hatch-vcs`** — `[tool.hatch.version] source = "vcs"`. The version is derived from `git describe`, so `pyproject`, the package, and releases **cannot drift** — the hand-synced literal (and the whole class of drift bug #87 started fixing) is gone. Between tags → dev version; on a tag → that tag (`v0.1.0` → `0.1.0`).
- **`__init__.py`** — `__version__` imports the build-generated `_version.py`, falling back to `importlib.metadata` in a raw source tree, then a sentinel. Never a literal. (`multiproxy` serverInfo + `cli --version` both consume it.)
- **`release.yml`** — on a `v*` tag push: build sdist+wheel, **assert the built version matches the tag**, publish a GitHub Release with generated notes + artifacts. Actions SHA-pinned. **No PyPI** (git-install-first; `terse` is taken on PyPI — publishing would force a rename, still deferred).
- **`tests.yml`** — `fetch-depth: 0` (hatch-vcs needs full history) + SHA-pinned actions for consistency.
- `_version.py` gitignored; mypy override since it's generated and may be absent at check time.

## Why

`terse` had zero release tags and two hand-synced version declarations. This makes releases reproducible and the version un-drift-able by construction, using `uv tool install git+<repo>@<tag>` as the install path.

## Verification (local)

- `uv build` → hatch-vcs resolves a version (dev between tags), no error; `_version.py` generated + gitignored
- `terse.__version__` and `terse --version` both resolve; mypy passes **with `_version.py` absent** (the fresh-CI condition)
- ruff + mypy clean; **554 tests pass**; both workflow files parse as valid YAML

## After merge

Cut `v0.1.0` on the merge commit → `release.yml` fires → first GitHub Release. (The workflow must exist at the tagged commit, hence tag-after-merge.)

title: version: single-source from __init__.py + add `terse --version`
body:
## What

First slice of the smart-install / versioning workstream — the **safe, non-protected core**. No git tags, no CI changes, no publish surface.

- **Single source of version truth.** `pyproject.toml` now declares `dynamic = ["version"]` and hatchling reads `__version__` from `src/terse/__init__.py` via `[tool.hatch.version]`. The static `version = "0.0.1"` is removed. Previously the version lived in *both* files, hand-synced — a drift bug waiting to ship a mismatched install.
- **`terse --version`** — was missing entirely (`terse --version` errored). Added a root-parser flag that reads the installed dist via `importlib.metadata`, falling back to the package `__version__` for source-tree runs.

## Why

`terse` is a public repo with zero release tags and a broken `--version`. This lands the irreducible hygiene that every later install path (git-install, and eventually PyPI) builds on — without taking on any protected/irreversible decision.

## Verification

- `terse --version` → `terse 0.0.1`; `python -m terse --version` (fallback path) → `terse 0.0.1`
- `uv build` → `terse-0.0.1.whl` + `.tar.gz`, version sourced from `__init__.py` (build would error if it still wanted the static value)
- `ruff` + `mypy` clean (29 files); **554 tests pass**

## Deferred (Protected — separate step)

Tag-derived versioning (`hatch-vcs`, tag = version), the tag-triggered GitHub Release CI, and any PyPI publish (which would force a rename — `terse` is taken on PyPI). These need git tags + CI and are held for explicit sign-off.

title: policy: per-rule "capture": false — never persist a tool's payloads (#85)
body:
Closes #85. Unblocks safely wrapping a credential-returning MCP server.

## The gap

`--capture-dir` tees **every** raw payload to disk before compression, **ungated by policy** (the tee sat above the tier logic and never consulted `policy.select`). `--debug-log` records embed the raw payload too. So terse could not express *"compress this server, but never persist that tool"* — which is exactly what blocked wrapping `secret-broker`, whose `secret_reveal_credential` returns a plaintext credential **by design**.

The trap worth naming: **`"tiers": []` does not help.** Passthrough correctly keeps no in-memory diff base, so it *looks* like the safe answer — but the capture tee runs before the tiers and would still write the payload to disk. Easy to assume otherwise.

## The fix

A rule may carry `"capture": false`, gating **both** disk sinks:

```jsonc
{ "match": { "tool": "secret-broker.*" },
  "tiers": [],          // no compression, no diff base
  "capture": false }    // ...and never write it to disk
```

- **Both sinks, not one** — the audit/replay log embeds `blocks:[{raw, emitted}]`, so gating only the corpus tee would be half a guard (the open question from #85; resolved to yes).
- **The ledger still counts it** — sizes + decision, never content (#82), so a gated tool is measured, just never quoted. Losing the row would be a needless blind spot.
- **Client-invisible** — what the client receives is byte-identical, pinned by a test.
- **Default `true`** — pre-#85 behavior exactly.

### Strict parsing is the load-bearing detail

`_coerce_capture` raises on a non-bool rather than coercing. Every wrong-typed value in Python is truthy — **`bool("false") is True`** — so a lax coercion (the `bool(...)` the non-security knobs use) would silently turn the guard back **ON**, writing the very payloads it exists to keep off disk. That is the one direction a typo must never fail in, so it fails loudly at load, before a single payload is proxied. Parametrized over `"false" / "no" / 0 / 1 / None / [] / {}`.

`"capture"` is registered in `_RULE_KEYS` — strict-key validation (#77) would otherwise reject any policy using it. Pinned by a test, since the guard must be spelled exactly this way to work at all.

## Why not just omit `--capture-dir` for that server?

It works today, and it is the right move right now. But the safety then rests on **remembering** never to pass the flag to one particular wrapper — the same failure shape as the codegraph `node@22` pin: an invariant in a human's head, one re-install away from vanishing. It also cannot be expressed under `--config` multiproxy at all (one process, N peers, one `capture_dir`).

This makes it declarative: durable across re-wraps, reviewable next to every other decision, and per-peer under multiproxy.

## Verification

- **554 passed** (14 new), ruff clean, mypy clean; `policy.example.json` still loads with capture defaults intact.
- **Live trace** — real proxy, `capture: false`, **both** sinks explicitly enabled (`--capture-dir` *and* `--debug-log`):
  - client received the payload: **yes** (the guard is functionally free)
  - corpus files written: **0** · audit log: **not written** · payload found anywhere on disk: **0 files**
  - ledger still recorded it: `{"decision":"passthrough","raw_tokens":609}` — counted, not quoted.
- A sibling tool in the **same proxy** still captures normally — proving the gate is per-rule, not per-proxy.

## Follow-up (not in this PR)

With the guard structural, wrapping `secret-broker` becomes safe by construction. The honest cost/benefit still stands though: its compression upside is ~zero (tiny payloads), so the case for wrapping it is uniformity, not savings.

title: policy: per-rule "capture": false — make "never persist this tool's payloads" declarative, not remembered
body:
Blocks safely wrapping a credential-returning MCP server (secret-broker) behind terse.

## The gap

`--capture-dir` tees **every** text block of **every** result to disk, before compression, **ungated by policy** (`proxy.py:334` — the tee sits above the tier logic and never consults `policy.select`). So today there is no way to express *"compress this server, but never persist that tool's payloads."*

This is the one thing standing between terse and wrapping `secret-broker`, whose `secret_reveal_credential` returns a plaintext credential **by design**. Wrapping it with the same flags as every other server would write live credentials into `~/.config/terse/session-corpus`.

Note what does NOT help:
- **`"tiers": []` (passthrough)** — correctly keeps no in-memory diff base (`"hands off entirely, no state kept"`), but does **not** stop the capture tee. Easy to assume it would.
- **The ledger** — already payload-free (#82), so it is not the risk. It would happily measure `secret-broker` today.

## Why "just omit --capture-dir" is not the answer

It works — `install-mcp` gives each server its own proxy, so per-server capture control already exists, and that is the correct move *right now*. But the safety then rests entirely on **remembering** never to pass `--capture-dir` to that one wrapper. That is the exact failure shape as the codegraph `node@22` PATH pin: an invariant held in a human's head, one re-install away from vanishing. A future `install-mcp secret-broker --capture-dir ...` — or a copy-pasted command — silently starts writing credentials to disk, with nothing to catch it.

It also cannot be expressed at all under `--config` multiproxy: one process, N peers, one `capture_dir`. There is no way to exclude a single peer.

## Proposal

A per-rule `"capture": false`, so the guard lives in the policy file — surviving re-wraps, applying to multiproxy, and reviewable in one place.

```jsonc
{
  "match": { "tool": "secret-broker.*" },
  "tiers": [],          // no compression, no diff base
  "capture": false      // ...and never tee the payload to disk
}
```

Sketch:
- `Rule.capture: bool = True` (default preserves today’s behavior exactly).
- `load_policy`: add `"capture"` to `_RULE_KEYS` (policy.py:139 — strict-key validation already rejects unknown keys, so this must be registered or a policy using it fails loudly; that strictness is a feature here).
- `proxy.py:334`: gate the tee on `self.policy.select(tool, self.server_name).capture`.
- Test that a `capture: false` rule writes nothing to the corpus dir while a sibling tool in the same proxy still captures.
- Docs: USAGE (the secret-broker recipe) + TECHNICAL (policy schema).

Then `secret-broker` becomes safe **by construction** rather than by discipline, and the recipe is a policy rule instead of a remembered flag omission.

## Worth deciding as part of this

Should `capture: false` also imply the `--debug-log` replay trace is skipped for that tool? It embeds raw payloads too (`blocks:[{raw, emitted}]`), so it has the identical exposure. Leaning **yes** — one declaration should cover "never persist this tool's content anywhere", or the guard is only half a guard.

## Value, honestly

The compression upside for `secret-broker` is ~zero (tiny payloads — `list_credentials` returns a short list). Nobody should wrap it for the savings. The value here is **structural**: making "this tool's output must never hit disk" a first-class, declarative property of a policy rather than an operator's memory. Discovered while deciding whether to wrap secret-broker (verified on terse @ 7bfc6fa).

title: policy: make server-scoped rules match servers that don't self-prefix (#83)
body:
Closes #83. Found by the savings ledger (#82) on its first day of production traffic.

## The bug

A policy rule like `runecho.*` is scoping to a **server** — but MCP tool names don't inherently carry one. kb names its own tools `kb.read.search`, so `kb.*` matched unaided and its "drop dictionary" tuning was live. runecho calls its tool plain `structure`, so `runecho.*` matched nothing, fell through to `defaults`, and **nothing said so**. Whether a server-scoped glob worked at all depended on whether the downstream happened to self-prefix its tool names — which is the actual root cause; the two dead rules in `policy.example.json` were just where it surfaced.

Latent rather than broken, because both dead rules happened to specify the same tiers as `defaults`. The trap was that editing them would have had no effect, silently.

## The fix

`Policy.select(tool, server=None)` / `apply(..., server=None)` try a synthesized `{server}.{bare tool}` candidate **first**, so a server-scoped rule means the same thing for every server.

- **Additive** — every pre-existing candidate is still tried in its original order, so a policy that matched before matches the same rule now (pinned by a test asserting `select(t) == select(t, server=None)` across the existing cases).
- **Qualified outranks bare** — a server-scoped rule is the more specific intent, mirroring how multiproxy's peer-qualified candidate already outranks its bare fallback.
- **No double-qualification** — `kb.read.search` under server `kb` stays itself, not `kb.kb.read.search` (which would have *broken* the one rule that already worked).

Wiring: `proxy --server-name NAME`; `install-mcp` bakes it in from the MCP config's own server name; multiproxy passes each peer's `name`; `--server-name` with `--config` is rejected loudly (a single flag can't name N peers — same reasoning as `--header`).

Also fixes the adjacent papercut from the same root: the ledger's `server` field now uses the given name, so kb stops labeling itself `sb-run` (the secret-broker launcher's basename).

Docs: TECHNICAL.md matching/candidate order + the `stats.py` label note, USAGE.md ("Tell terse which server it's fronting" + the policy-adjustment caveat), and a `_comment_server_scope` in `policy.example.json` next to the rules that motivated it.

## Verification

- **540 passed** (11 new), ruff clean, mypy clean. Three existing install-mcp/cli assertions updated for the newly-baked flag.
- **Live trace** — real proxy, policy `{"runecho.*": {"tiers": []}}`, bare tool name: with `--server-name runecho` the ledger records `"decision": "passthrough"` and the result is forwarded untouched (pre-fix: rule missed, defaults compressed it). Same run confirms `"server": "runecho"` instead of the `python` basename.
- `policy.example.json` still loads; `select('structure', 'runecho')` now resolves to its rule.

title: Server-scoped policy rules (runecho.*, codegraph.*) are unmatchable for single-server proxies — silently fall through to defaults
body:
## What the live ledger surfaced

The new savings ledger (#82) records the tool name the proxy actually matched policy against. Its first production rows show:

```
{"server": "runecho-mcp", "tool": "structure", "decision": "unchanged", ...}
{"server": "runecho-mcp", "tool": "health", "decision": "unchanged", ...}
```

The tool name arrives **bare** (`structure`), not server-qualified (`runecho.structure`). But `policy.example.json` — and my live `~/.config/terse/policy.json` — author rules as:

```json
{ "match": { "tool": "runecho.*" }, "tiers": ["minify", "tabularize", "dictionary"] },
{ "match": { "tool": "codegraph.*" }, "tiers": ["minify", "tabularize", "dictionary"] },
```

Those globs **never match**. `Policy.select` (policy.py:73) matches `tool` as given, then — only if it contains `PREFIX_SEP` (`__`, the multiproxy peer prefix) — the bare part after the separator. A single-server proxy's tool name has no prefix and no server component, so `fnmatch("structure", "runecho.*")` is False and the rule falls through to `defaults`.

## Why it's latent rather than broken today

Both dead rules happen to specify the same tiers as `defaults`, so current behavior is identical and nothing looks wrong. The trap is that **editing them has no effect and nothing says so** — e.g. copying the `kb.*` rule's "drop dictionary" tuning onto `runecho.*` would silently do nothing.

`kb.*` genuinely matches, because kb names its own tools `kb.read.recent_sessions` — the server component is part of the tool name at the source, not added by terse. That inconsistency is the actual root cause: **whether a server-scoped glob works depends entirely on whether the downstream server happens to self-prefix its tool names.**

## Adjacent papercut (same root)

The ledger's `server` field is the downstream command's basename, so kb records as `sb-run` (the secret-broker wrapper it launches through) rather than `kb`. Accurate, but opaque in `terse stats` output.

## Possible fixes (not yet chosen)

1. **`install-mcp` bakes the config's server name into the wrapper** (e.g. `--server-name runecho`), so the proxy can qualify tool names for policy matching and label the ledger with the real server identity. Fixes both halves; costs a new flag + a re-wrap to take effect.
2. **Validate at load**: warn when a rule glob can't match any tool the proxy sees. Cheap, catches the trap without changing semantics — but only after traffic flows.
3. **Document it**: state plainly that server-scoped globs only work for self-prefixing servers, and key rules on bare tool names otherwise. Zero code, zero enforcement.

Leaning 1 + 3 — 1 makes the intent work, 3 covers hand-authored policies. Verified on terse @ 5bbb8f8.

title: live savings ledger + terse stats: payload-free per-result records, default-on
body:
## What

Closes the "proven value coverage" gap: terse could measure savings over a synthetic corpus (`terse measure`) and validate model fluency, but the live proxies produced zero visibility into real-session savings — the only production trace, `--debug-log`, embeds raw payloads so it is never left on. "How many tokens did terse save me this week?" was unanswerable.

- **`src/terse/stats.py` (new):** payload-free ledger. One JSONL record per tool result — ts, server, tool, decision (`compressed`/`diff`/`unchanged`/`passthrough`, derived by sniffing the emitted envelope head, no state threaded out of the compression paths), char + cl100k token sizes. **Never payload content** — the property that makes always-on safe where capture/audit must stay opt-in. Rotates at 10MB (one generation kept); O_APPEND single-line writes stay atomic across concurrent proxies; a torn tail from a crashed writer is self-healed (found by the new tests — it used to poison the next good record).
- **Proxy integration:** `Interceptor(stats=...)` callback with the exact capture/audit contract (side-effect only, failures swallowed, can never change what the client receives). `run_proxy(stats_log=...)`; multiproxy wires a per-peer writer so the ledger's `server` field is the peer's config name.
- **Default-ON in the CLI** (`terse proxy`): resolved to `$XDG_STATE_HOME/terse/stats.jsonl`; `--no-stats` disables, `--stats-log FILE` redirects, `install-mcp --no-stats` bakes the opt-out. The `run_proxy`/`run_multi_proxy` API default stays `None` (disabled) so library callers opt in explicitly. Editable installs pick this up on next session restart — no re-wrap needed.
- **`terse stats [--log] [--since 30m|24h|7d] [--json]` (new command):** totals, decision mix (diff hit-rate), per-server/per-tool rows. Token totals sum only fully-tokenized records; untokenized ones are counted and reported, never blended with char sizes.
- **`tests/conftest.py`:** autouse XDG_STATE_HOME isolation so no test can ever write the real ledger.

## Verification

- 529 passed (36 new), ruff clean, mypy clean.
- Live end-to-end: real `terse proxy` run against the fake server wrote the default-path ledger — first call `compressed` (609→208 tok), repeat call `diff` (609→97 tok) — and `terse stats` reported 75% saved plus correct `--since`/`--json` output.

title: docs: drop stale Phase-0/spike framing, acknowledge TOON as prior art
body:
Resurrects orphaned local branch commit 2af1e6e (2026-07-03) that was never PR'd — found in a repo sweep. Re-derived against current main rather than cherry-picked: the original predates #73 (Anthropic counter removal) and #76 (diff default-on), so the affected wording was rewritten to match today's facts.

- README + TECHNICAL: prior-art paragraph acknowledging TOON publishes the same tabularization primitive; terse's claim is the bundle (MCP-native proxy, per-tool policy, cross-call diffing, drop-to-retrieve, behavioral fluency gate), not the primitive.
- `__init__.py`: module docstring no longer says capture/probes/report/cli are "stubbed to the plan" — rewritten to the current module map (measurement spine / operational shell / behavioral evals).
- Remaining "spike" framing swept from capture/cli/policy/probes/tokenize docstrings, README Status, and the report/HTML report titles ("terse spike report" → "terse measurement report").
- Deliberately NOT renamed: the `reports/spike-report.md` default output path — that's a behavior change existing scripts may reference.

Verification: 497 tests, ruff, mypy all green; `grep -rn spike src/ *.md tests/` returns only the kept default path.

title: codec hot-path fix (#79) + fluency package split (#78)
body:
Closes #79. Closes #78. Two commits, one per issue.

## Commit 1 — codec hot path (#79)

**Quadratic re-minification.** `_count_value_nodes` and `_replace_nodes` called `minify(node)` at every container level — the whole subtree re-serialized per level, in both walks. Replaced with one bottom-up canonicalization pass (`_build_canon_memo`) that assembles each container's minified form once from its children's already-built forms, memoized by `id(node)`; both walks and `compress_with`'s base are now memo lookups.

- Byte-identical to `json.dumps` by construction, pinned by a property test over unicode/floats/escapes/empty containers **and non-str dict keys** (the coercion trap: those fall back to `json.dumps` for that node).
- A call-count regression test asserts `minify` is no longer called per container level.
- Measured: ~4.3× on a depth-150 payload (11.1→2.6 ms); wide 400KB record payloads ~8% (they're tokenizer-bound, not serialization-bound).

**Depth cap.** `capture.py`'s private `_MAX_SHAPE_DEPTH = 200` is now the codec-wide `transforms.MAX_DEPTH`, with an iterative `exceeds_depth()` checked at every boundary:

- `policy.apply` — too-deep payload passes through untouched, with a warning (same contract as the reserved-marker guard).
- proxy `_compress_or_diff` — a too-deep payload is never stored as a diff base (the diff encoders deep-compare recursively).
- `measure.measure_payload` — reports the row non-applicable instead of RecursionError-ing `terse measure` (the crash in the issue).
- `json.loads` RecursionError (Python 3.11 floor; 3.12+ parses iteratively) caught alongside JSONDecodeError at each of those boundaries.

Behavior change to note: payloads nested deeper than 200 levels are now passed through uncompressed rather than compressed-if-under-the-Python-recursion-limit. That matches what the shape classifier already considered too deep, and no real tool output approaches it.

Alternative considered and rejected: Merkle/structural hashing (true O(n)) — a hash collision would silently corrupt the legend on the live path, where the round-trip gate doesn't run per payload. The memo keeps exact-string keys.

## Commit 2 — fluency package split (#78)

Pure move of the 958-line five-job module into `src/terse/fluency/`:

| module | contents |
|---|---|
| `questions.py` | the three question sources behind `gen_questions`, + text-diff's |
| `scoring.py` | programmatic answer checking (`score`, `_score_form`) |
| `answerers.py` | `Answerer` protocol + the stdlib-urllib live backend |
| `harnesses.py` | paired runners: plain / diff / chain-soak / text-diff |
| `pack.py` | offline eval packs + `PRIMER` |

The package `__init__` re-exports the entire old module namespace — including the private helpers `dropeval.py` reuses by design and the `fluency.text_diff` / `fluency.compress` attributes tests patch/call — so cli, report, dropeval, and the whole suite run with **zero test edits**. An AST diff of the old module's top-level names vs the new facade shows nothing missing.

## Verification

- `uv run pytest -q` — 497 passed (both commits individually and at tip)
- `uv run ruff check .` / `uv run mypy` — clean
- `terse --help` / `terse fluency --help` smoke — OK

title: Codec hot-path costs: dict_encode re-minifies every container (quadratic) + no recursion depth cap outside the classifier
body: Audit 2026-07-14: (1) _replace_nodes/_count_value_nodes call minify(node) at every container level — quadratic on 100KB+ payloads (codegraph-scale) on the live proxy path. Consider caching canonical forms per node id or a single bottom-up pass. (2) capture has _MAX_SHAPE_DEPTH but transforms/measure don't — a pathologically nested payload RecursionErrors measure_payload (proxy is safe via fail-open). Add a shared depth cap.

title: Split fluency.py (958 lines, five jobs): question sources / scoring / harnesses
body: Audit 2026-07-14 finding: fluency.py now hosts three question generators, scoring, the answerer transport, pack building, and four eval harnesses. Nothing is wrong individually, but it's the module where the next feature lands in the wrong place by default. Split without behavior change; report/CLI imports stay stable.

title: audit fixes: checked types + curated lint; install-mcp drift guard; strict policy keys; transport credential guard
body:
Three fixes from the 2026-07-14 module-by-module audit, approved as a batch.

## 1. Annotations → checked contract
- `[tool.ruff.lint]` curated select (`I,B,UP,C4,SIM,RET,PLE` on top of defaults) — the repo previously ran ruff's minimal defaults with no config at all.
- `[tool.mypy]` lenient baseline (ratchet later), added to the CI lint job; `mypy` in dev deps.
- Burn-down: 114 ruff findings (96 auto-fixed; 18 manual with explicit `zip(..., strict=)` chosen per real semantics at each site). 17 mypy errors, several genuinely load-bearing:
  - `pump()` claimed `src: TextIO` but receives an `Iterator[str]` from `HttpTransport` → now `Iterable[str]` (the actual contract);
  - `answer_retrieve` could index the drop store with a non-`str` handle from a malformed call → explicit miss;
  - an invalid `# type: ignore` comment in `_emit_audit` (trailing prose broke the syntax) was suppressing nothing → replaced with real narrowing.

## 2. install-mcp drift guard
A re-wrap rebuilt the entry purely from the stashed original, silently reverting hand-edits to the wrapped entry — this reverted codegraph's node@22 `env.PATH` pin in production on 07-13. `wrap()` now carries live non-terse-owned keys forward (`command`/`args` always rebuilt; `url`/`headers` never resurrected from a drifted entry), `do_install` reports the preserved keys, the CLI prints them with the uninstall caveat, USAGE updated. Regression test reproduces the codegraph scenario exactly.

## 3. Hardening pair
- **`load_policy` rejects unknown keys at every level** (`_`-prefixed annotations exempt — the `policy_gen` convention). It immediately caught a latent specimen in our own tree: a multiproxy test's policy file used `"rules"` (schema says `"policies"`) and the old loader swallowed it.
- **`HttpTransport` refuses credential-bearing headers over cleartext http to a remote host** — parity with `fluency.openai_answerer`'s existing TLS guard. Loopback gateways and https unaffected.

## Verification
- **476 tests pass** (+5), `ruff` clean at the new bar, `mypy` clean.
- The **live production policy file loads under the strict loader** (verified against a real proxy launch) — no deployment impact.

title: flip cross-call diffing to default-on; add --no-diff opt-outs
body:
## Why

The validation program that kept `--diff` opt-in is **complete**: pair fluency (4-model panel, 100%), the nested-record surface (#72: `structure` diff 100% vs full-terse 94%), and the drift soak (#75: mechanical zero-drift at depth 300 with an independent client-side reconstructor; behavioral depth-1..5 PASS on real corpus chains — overall worst gap −2% ±1, depth-5 gap −1% ±2). The default should match the measured reality; the opt-in stance was a validation gate, not a design preference.

## What

- **`Policy.diff` defaults `True`**; `load_policy` matches. An explicit policy-file `"diff": false` is still honored.
- **proxy: tri-state CLI** — `--diff` forces on (only meaningful against an opting-out policy file), new **`--no-diff`** forces off, neither keeps the policy value. Mutually exclusive (exit 2). `multiproxy`'s `diff_override` becomes `Optional[bool]` (`None` = leave each peer's policy alone), so both flags stay proxy-wide including peers with their own `policy_path`.
- **install-mcp: tri-state `diff`** — a plain wrap writes **no** diff flag (the entry inherits the proxy default); `--diff`/`--no-diff` bake an explicit override into the entry; `--diff-keyframe-interval` no longer requires `--diff` (and is dropped with `--no-diff`, where it would be dead weight). Flags still always reflect the latest install invocation.
- **Docs**: README tier/Status; TECHNICAL's diff bullet rewritten (opt-in rationale is now history; the unobservable context-compaction residual keeps `--no-diff` as the escape hatch); USAGE proxy/install-mcp/text-diff sections, incl. the stash hand-edit gotcha found during the #74 rollout.

## Verification

- **471 tests pass**, `ruff` clean. The two off-by-default pins became on-by-default + `"diff": false` opt-out pins (JSON and CDC-text paths); install-mcp tri-state covered (`None` drops flags, `False` bakes `--no-diff`).
- **Live smoke** (runecho downstream): a **flagless** proxy diffs the repeat `structure` call (117,587 → 205 chars); `--no-diff` sends the full form both times.

title: diff drift soak: mechanical long-chain pytest + fluency --diff-soak depth eval
body:
## Why

The #8/#20 follow-up gating any `--diff` default-flip. Per-hop losslessness is proven at encode time and unit-pinned, but nothing exercised **drift**: (1) does client-side reconstruction stay exact hundreds of chained hops deep, across keyframes, shape flips, and reconnects; (2) does **model comprehension** degrade as consecutive diffs chain off one full anchor (production allows up to `diff_keyframe_interval` = 5 before re-anchoring).

## Mechanical half — `tests/test_diff_soak.py`

Drives the real `Interceptor` with an independent client-side reconstructor (`ModelView`, built only on the public decode functions — it never peeks at proxy state), asserting reconstruction == raw payload at **every hop**:

- **400 interleaved hops** across three evolving workloads (record lists, structure-like dict-maps, text logs) with non-JSON error interludes (#8 base eviction) and a mid-soak client reconnect (#20 reset) — post-error and post-reconnect emissions verified to re-anchor as fulls.
- **300-diff unbounded chain** (`interval 0`) — exact at depth 300.
- **200-step text-only chain** (CDC codec keeps its own base/keyframe state).
- Keyframe cadence ≤5 verified throughout. **Zero drift found.**

## Behavioral half — `terse fluency --diff-soak`

Builds real depth-1..K chain windows from the corpus (chronological, every hop must admit a lossless diff), scores the same final-state questions as (full anchor + k wires) vs a full-terse control — no system primer, production condition — and reports accuracy **by depth** with worst-model verdicts overall and at the deepest depth (`build_diff_soak_report`; `--soak-depth`/`--soak-windows` to size it).

**Enabler:** `_flat_record_questions` — fluency-local questions for single flat-record payloads (keys-count + deterministic scalar lookups). kb payloads are per-block flat dicts that generated zero questions, leaving the deepest real chains untestable; same fluency-local pattern as #72's nested-record source, codec/tabularizer rules untouched.

## Live result (this PR's gate)

Loopback gateway, deepseek-v4-flash + glm-5.2, 3 trials, ~50 real-corpus windows, depths 1–5:

| gate | worst model | chain | full | gap | verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| overall | deepseek-v4-flash | 94% | 96% | −2% ±1 | **PASS** (5% tol) |
| depth 5 (production bound) | deepseek-v4-flash | 96% | 97% | −1% ±2 | **PASS** (5% tol) |

No depth-correlated drift: the depth-3 dip (−6/−8%) hits both models on the same windows and recovers at depths 4–5 — window-specific, not accumulation.

## Verification

- **471 tests pass** (+8: 3 soak, 5 fluency incl. flat-record pins), `ruff` clean.
- Docs: USAGE (new `--diff-soak` section), TECHNICAL (soak note on the diff limitation bullet), README (status line).

title: install-mcp: opt-in --diff wiring; docs: diff fluency is now validated
body:
## Why

The #63→#72 diff-validation arc **passed** (4-model panel 100% on the record surface; `structure` diff-fluency 100% vs 94% full-terse) — but the harvest never happened: the live wrappers run without `--diff`, `install-mcp` had no way to enable it, and the CLI help / docs still said *"fluency unverified"*, which is now false.

Evidence from the live session corpus (972 real payloads from the wrapped runecho/codegraph/kb servers):

- Tier-0 saves **+3.4%** overall on real traffic (27.2K of 805K tokens); all 972/972 round-trip losslessly.
- `codegraph_explore` alone is **202K tokens (25% of live traffic) at +0.0% saved** (long-text shape — tier-0 can't touch it).
- The probe shows **25–73% cross-call overlap** on successive `codegraph_explore` payloads, 95%+ on several kb tools — exactly what the diff tier eats.

## What

- `install_mcp.wrap()` / `do_install()` gain `diff` + `diff_keyframe_interval`; a wrapped entry carries `--diff [--diff-keyframe-interval K]` before the `--` separator. Flags always reflect the **latest** install invocation — a re-install without `--diff` drops it (never accumulates), same contract as `--capture-dir`/policy.
- CLI: `install-mcp --diff` / `--diff-keyframe-interval K`; the install summary prints the diff state.
- Stale wording fixed everywhere it claimed diff fluency was unverified: `proxy --diff` help, the `install_mcp` module docstring, README (tier list + Status), USAGE (install-mcp section).

## Verification

- **460 tests pass** (base 458; +2: flag placement, keyframe-rides-with-diff, `do_install` threading, drop-on-reinstall), `ruff` clean.
- **Live smoke** (`proxy --diff` fronting runecho): first `structure` call returned the full compressed payload (117,587 chars); the repeat call arrived as a 205-char `__terse_diff__`.
- `install-mcp --diff --print` dry-run against the real `~/.claude.json` shows the expected before/after for runecho/codegraph/kb, writes nothing.

title: chore: remove all Anthropic API integration (never used)
body:
Removes all Anthropic API integration from terse — it was never used, so the code
paths were dead weight that implied a workflow we don't run.

## Removed

- **`fluency.anthropic_answerer`** + **`dropeval.anthropic_tool_answerer`** (and the
  `_to_anthropic_tool` / `_to_anthropic_messages` adapters) — the "real consumer" eval
  arm. The fluency/drop evals reach **every** model over the OpenAI-compatible path
  (broker pool or a loopback gateway); there's no other model backend.
- **`tokenize.count_anthropic`** + **`measure --anthropic`** — the `count_tokens`
  "ground truth" check. cl100k stays the estimate; **cross-tokenizer (cl100k vs o200k)
  invariance is the keyless robustness check**, unchanged.
- The **`--anthropic` / `--anthropic-model`** CLI flags, the Anthropic section of the
  measure report, the **`anthropic` optional extra** (pruned from `uv.lock` with its
  transitive deps: httpx/anyio/distro/…), and the doc + `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` env mentions.
- Also: stop tracking a machine-local `.codegraph` symlink and gitignore it.

## Verification

- **452 tests pass**, `ruff` clean, `uv lock --check` consistent.
- `terse fluency --help` / `terse measure --help` carry no `--anthropic` flag; all
  modules import.
- **No behavior change to any path terse actually uses** — the removed paths required an
  Anthropic key that was never present.

Independent of #71/#72 (fluency nested-record coverage); this is pure removal.

title: fluency: cover nested-record tools (structure) so proxy --diff is validated (#71)
body:
Closes #71.

## Problem

`terse fluency --diff` (the gate for `proxy --diff`) generated **zero questions for
`runecho.structure`** — so the tool where the diff tier's value actually lives
(72–96% cross-call overlap, ~all of the measured +31.7% savings) was **behaviorally
unvalidated**, even though the harness reported a clean PASS on `locate` alone.

**Root cause** (corrected from the issue's original write-up — `extract_records` *does*
recurse into lists): `runecho.structure` is a **dict-map keyed by path**
(`files{"a/x.py": {symbols:[…]}}`), and `symbols[]` is **non-uniform** — functions carry
`{name,kind,line,hash}`, imports only `{name,kind}`. `_uniform_dict_list` requires an
identical key set, so the list is skipped → no records → no questions.

## Fix

Fluency-local `_nested_record_group` + `_nested_questions`:

- Reach records in a dict-map of parent records (structure's `files{}→symbols[]`).
- Generate **group-scoped** `count`/`enumerate`/`lookup` (+ `aggregate` when a numeric
  column is shared) over the **intersection columns** (present in every record), scoped to
  the first file (`…under files["a/x.py"]…`) so non-uniform lists stay well-posed.
- **Preferred over the uniform extractor** for dict-map payloads — otherwise
  `extract_records` returns whichever single file's symbols happen to be uniform, yielding
  an ambiguous *unscoped* "how many records" the model can't answer against a multi-file map.
- **Does NOT touch the shared `extract_records`/`_uniform_dict_list`** — the tabularizer,
  probe, and drop-path logic (#47) still see the strict rule unchanged. This only widens
  what the fluency harness can ASK about.

## Verification

- **457 tests pass** (452 + 5 new nested-path tests).
- Real `structure` payloads now generate 3 questions each.
- **Structure diff-fluency runs and PASSES**: deepseek-v4-flash, 0 regressions
  (diff 100% vs full-terse 94% at n=6×3). Combined with the earlier 4-model panel on
  `locate` (100%/100%), the diff format is fluent on both the low- and high-value surfaces.

## Scope

Coverage gap only. The `proxy --diff` default-flip / per-tool policy heuristic is a
deliberate **follow-up** — this PR just makes that decision measurable.

title: Fluency eval generates no questions for nested-record tools (runecho.structure) — the high-value diff surface is unvalidated
body:
**Value: high · Effort: M · evidence-gated (measured, not assumed)**

## Summary

The cross-call **diff fluency** eval (`terse fluency --diff`, the gate for `proxy --diff`)
generates **zero questions for nested-record tools** — most importantly `runecho.structure`.
So the one tool where the diff tier's value actually lives is **behaviorally unvalidated**,
even though the harness reports a clean PASS.

## What was measured (real runecho corpus, this repo's own dogfood)

A live measure + probe + diff-fluency pass over 26 raw runecho payloads across 5 real repos:

- **Default lossless win is real: +31.7%** (cl100k), ~all of it from the dictionary tier
  (Tier 0.5). `minify`/`tabularize` are ~0 on this output — runecho already emits compact JSON.
- **Cross-call overlap is large**: consecutive same-tool `structure` payloads overlap **72–96%**
  (same repo); `status` ~60%; `locate` up to 83%. This is the diff tier's headroom.
- **Diff fluency PASSES** across a diverse 4-model panel (deepseek-v4-pro, glm-5.2, qwen3.7-max,
  kimi-k2.6) at 5 trials: **60/60 full and 60/60 diff for every model — zero regressions.**
  (A regression on `enumerate`/`aggregate` seen only on the *cheapest* model, deepseek-v4-flash,
  did **not** reproduce on any stronger model — it was model-capability, not a format flaw.)

**The catch:** every one of those questions was `runecho.locate`. `structure`, `status`, and
`health` produced **0 questions**, so the panel never actually tested a `structure` diff — the
72–96%-overlap, +31.7%-savings surface.

## Root cause (verified, not hypothesized)

`gen_questions()` → `extract_records()` → `find_record_list_with_path()` **walks dict keys only
and returns the first uniform-dict list**; by its own docstring it does not descend into list
elements ("a record list nested inside another list has no simple expressible path").

Empirically, per tool in the corpus:

| tool | top-level keys | `extract_records` | questions |
|---|---|---|---|
| `runecho.locate` | `…, symbols, …` | 23 recs (`name,kind,file,line,hash`) | 4 ✅ |
| `runecho.structure` | `…, files, …` | **None** | **0** ❌ |
| `runecho.status` | scalar object | None | 0 |
| `runecho.health` | scalar object | None | 0 |

`locate` works because its records sit at a **top-level** `symbols` key. `structure`'s real
records are the **per-file `symbols[]`**, nested inside `files[]` (a list) — one list-level
below where the walker stops. So `extract_records` returns `None` and no questions are generated.
Any tool whose records live one list deep hits the same wall.

## Impact

- The `proxy --diff` fluency gate gives a **false sense of coverage**: green, but only on the
  low-value tool. Defaulting `--diff` on for `structure`-shaped tools is currently **unvalidated**.
- Generalizes beyond runecho: any MCP server that returns grouped/nested records (parent → child
  arrays) is invisible to the fluency harness.

## Proposed fix

Add a **fluency-specific nested-record extractor** so `gen_questions` can reach records nested
one list-level deep (e.g. `structure.files[].symbols[]`), then emit the same
count/lookup/enumerate/aggregate questions against a chosen group.

Design notes / guardrails:
- **Do not change `extract_records`/`find_record_list_with_path` globally.** They are shared by
  `measure`, `probe`, `policy_gen`, and drop-path building (#47), where the "no simple expressible
  path for a list-nested record" rule is load-bearing. Keep the nested traversal in a fluency-local
  helper so the drop-path semantics are untouched.
- Keep generation **fully deterministic** (the reproducibility invariant): pick the target group
  and record by a fixed rule (e.g. first file whose `symbols[]` is non-empty; middle record).
- Enumerate/aggregate should scope to a **named group** ("…across the symbols of file X") so ground
  truth stays programmatically checkable and the question is well-posed for a nested shape.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `terse fluency --diff --corpus <runecho corpus>` generates ≥1 question per `structure` pair.
2. Re-run the 4-model panel (or `--anthropic` if a key is available) on `structure` diffs; report
   full-terse vs diff accuracy per model.
3. If `structure` diffs pass → the `proxy --diff` default (or a per-tool policy heuristic for
   high-overlap record tools) becomes an evidence-backed decision. If they fail on
   enumerate/aggregate the way flash did → scope `--diff` to lookup-shaped consumption + rely on
   keyframe cadence (#8), and document it.

## Refs

Diff tier (`proxy --diff`), diff-desync guard (#20), keyframe cadence (#8), drop-path extraction (#47).
Reproduction lives in the measure/probe/fluency CLIs already shipped — this is a coverage gap in
`gen_questions`, not a new subsystem.

title: security: the three remaining red-team lows (cleartext key, symlink write, unpinned dep)
body:
The three remaining Lows from the `/redteam` pass, batched. All defensive; none change happy-path behavior.

## [Low] Cleartext Bearer key in the fluency eval tool
- **Where:** `fluency.py` `openai_answerer`
- **Issue:** Sent `Authorization: Bearer <key>` to any `base_url`, including `http://`, leaking the key in cleartext on the wire.
- **Fix:** Refuses `http` to a **non-loopback** host when a key is set (raises `ValueError` at construction). Loopback http (local LiteLLM/CCR gateway) and `https` are unaffected; `http` with no key is still allowed (nothing to leak).

## [Low] `_secure_io` followed symlinks on write
- **Where:** `_secure_io.py` `write_restricted` / `append_restricted`
- **Issue:** Opened the final path component without `O_NOFOLLOW`, so a pre-planted symlink in a terse-managed dir could redirect a secret-bearing write (config, backup, captured payload) onto an attacker-chosen target.
- **Fix:** Both opens add `O_NOFOLLOW` via `getattr(os, "O_NOFOLLOW", 0)` — a no-op on a platform lacking it rather than a crash. Guards the final component (the standard `O_NOFOLLOW` limitation).

## [Low] Unpinned `tiktoken`
- **Where:** `pyproject.toml`
- **Issue:** Bare `"tiktoken"` — a lockfile-less `pip install terse` could resolve to an arbitrarily old release.
- **Fix:** Pinned a floor `tiktoken>=0.7`; `uv.lock` still pins the exact version for this repo.

## Tests (+6)
- `openai_answerer`: rejects http+key to a remote host; allows loopback-http+key, https+key, and http+no-key.
- `_secure_io`: refuses to write/append through a symlink and leaves the target untouched (skipped where `O_NOFOLLOW` is absent).

**452 pass, ruff clean.** Completes the redteam remediation — no open findings remain.

title: security: restrict downstream URL schemes to http/https; prune config backups
body:
Two findings from a `/redteam` pass on the proxy/installer surface. Threat model: local dev tool, but a downstream target can originate from an **untrusted, repo-committed project-scoped `.mcp.json`** — so config-supplied URLs aren't purely operator input.

## [Medium] SSRF / local-file read via unrestricted URL scheme
- **Where:** `transport.py` `build_transport` → `HttpTransport._post` (`urllib.request.urlopen`)
- **Issue:** Any config target containing `"://"` was handed to `urlopen` with **no scheme allowlist**. `urllib` honors `file://`, `ftp://`, `data:`, `gopher://` — not just http/https. A downstream `url` of `file:///home/you/.ssh/id_rsa` was read and its contents enqueued as the downstream "reply," flowing straight into the model's context; an internal `http://169.254.169.254/…` URL is an SSRF primitive. terse *widened* the surface here vs. a compliant HTTP client, which wouldn't honor `file://`.
- **Fix:** `HttpTransport.__init__` rejects any scheme outside `http`/`https` at construction (before any I/O). `run_proxy` catches the `ValueError` as a clean config error (**exit 2**); `run_multi_proxy` already routes a bad-peer `ValueError` to exit 2.

## [Low] Config-backup secret sprawl
- **Where:** `install_mcp.py` `_backup`
- **Issue:** Every install/uninstall wrote a new `<cfg>.bak-<ts>` full-config copy (MCP `env` blocks hold API keys) and **never pruned** them — a rotated/revoked key lingered in old backups indefinitely.
- **Fix:** `_backup` keeps only the `_MAX_BACKUPS` (5) most-recent backups per config, pruning older by mtime (best-effort; `keep=0` disables).

## Tests (+10)
- URL-scheme rejection (`file`/`ftp`/`data`/`gopher`) at the `HttpTransport`, `build_transport`, and `run_proxy` (exit 2) layers; `http`/`https` still construct fine.
- Backup pruning keeps the window, disables at `keep=0`, and prunes on a real `do_install`.

**446 pass, ruff clean.** Both fixes are defensive and reversible; neither changes the happy-path proxy behavior.

Not fixed here (the three remaining Lows from the review — non-TLS Bearer in the fluency eval tool, `O_NOFOLLOW` on `_secure_io`, unpinned `tiktoken`): lower impact, left for a follow-up if wanted.

title: #64 Phase 2: broadcast/merge resources|prompts|ping + route reads
body:
## What

Closes the documented `multiproxy.py` fan-out gaps: previously **any** client method other than `initialize`/`tools/list` (broadcast) or `tools/call` (routed) was forwarded to **peer 0 only**, silently dropping N-1 peers' data. This implements the live half of #64 (Phase 2 — the cross-server-dictionary half stays shelved, since Phase 0 empirically disproved it).

## Behavior

| Method(s) | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `resources/list`, `resources/templates/list`, `prompts/list`, `ping` | peer-0 only | **broadcast + aggregate** (concat lists / union caps / one primer) |
| `prompts/get` | peer-0 only | **routed by `{peer}__` prefix** (mirrors `tools/call`) |
| `resources/read`, `resources/subscribe`, `resources/unsubscribe` | peer-0 only | **scatter-gather** — every peer asked, first success `result` wins |
| `completion/complete`, `logging/setLevel`, … | peer-0 only | unchanged (documented peer-0 fallback, loud on stderr) |

## Design notes

- **Prompts get a `{peer}__` name prefix** in the merged list so `prompts/get` routes exactly like `tools/call`. **Resources don't** — a `uri` has scheme structure a prefix would corrupt — so their reads use scatter-gather instead. That asymmetry is deliberate: it closes the "see a resource you then can't read" trap without mangling uris.
- **Scatter-gather** reuses the existing broadcast id-remap/collect/timeout machinery unchanged; aggregate vs scatter differ only in `_merge_broadcast`. First success `result` wins (arrival order); a peer that doesn't own the uri errors and is discarded; all-error surfaces the first error. On a uri owned by two peers, first-arriving success wins — a documented tie-break, not a silent one.
- **Scatter replies are forwarded verbatim** (bypass the peer Interceptor → resource reads aren't terse-compressed). Reads aren't the compression target, and diffing a payload picked non-deterministically across peers would be incorrect.
- Extracted `_dispatch_routed` so `tools/call` and `prompts/get` share the dead-peer-timeout arming with zero duplication.

## Tests

8 new + 1 updated (the now-broadcast `resources/list` no longer exercises the peer-0 fallback — switched that test to `completion/complete`, a real method that genuinely still falls through). Direct-`Router` unit tests cover: concat-without-prefix, prefix routing + strip, scatter first-success, all-error surfacing, ping, and unknown-prefix error.

**436 pass, ruff clean.**

title: #64: measurement infra — capture-order replay + probe value-overlap gate
body:
Two measurement-infrastructure fixes surfaced while validating #64 Phase 1. Independent of the (shelved) session-dictionary feature.

## 1. Capture in chronological order (`capture.py`)
`capture_payload` now stamps `captured_at` (ns, preserved on rewrite for idempotency) and `load_corpus` replays in that order instead of sha-alphabetical filename order. Any order-dependent replay (a cross-call codec where a value must be *defined* by an earlier payload to be *elided* by a later one) was otherwise measured against a sequence that never happened. Legacy envelopes with no `captured_at` sort first in filename order (prior behavior); order-independent measures are unaffected.

## 2. `probe --cross-server` must never BUILD on token overlap (`report.py`)
Phase 0 greenlit a cross-server **value** dictionary on a 20.9% **Lever B** figure — but Lever B is idf-weighted **subword-token** overlap, and a value dictionary can only elide whole **values**. #64 Phase 1 proved these are unrelated: on a valid corpus, that class of token overlap sat atop **zero** cross-server value overlap and realized **−17.7%**.

Fix: when Lever A (the shared whole-value increment — the only realizable gate) is blind, the verdict is now **INCONCLUSIVE**, never a fallback **BUILD** on Lever B. Docstring + report caption carry the caveat.

## Why (the #64 finding)
Two servers describing the same subject share vocabulary (both emit "Interceptor", "proxy", "src/terse") without sharing complete elidable values (runecho emits the *symbol* `Interceptor`; kb emits prose *containing* "Interceptor"). Token-overlap is not value-elision headroom. The session-dictionary feature itself is being shelved on this basis; these two fixes make the measurement tooling tell the truth next time.

## Tests
Full suite green (428). +3 tests: capture-order replay, captured_at idempotency, and a regression guard pinning "blind Lever A + high Lever B → INCONCLUSIVE, not BUILD".

title: #64 Phase 1 (stages 1-2, WIP): shared cross-peer session-dictionary codec + wiring seam
body:
> **DRAFT / WIP** — foundation only (stages 1-2 of 4). The feature is **dormant**: no flag, `sess_encode` is never called by the compress flow, so this merges with **zero behavior change**. Stages 3-4 (guards + opt-in flag + measure) follow before it does anything live.

## Context

Phase 1 of #64 — a **session-level legend spanning all gateway peers**, so a value that appears in kb *and* codegraph *and* runecho is defined once and referenced (definition elided) by every later payload. This is the cross-server compression a client's native multiplex can't do. Greenlit by Phase 0 (co-resident content overlap **20.9%**, verdict LEAN BUILD).

## What's in this PR (stages 1-2)

**Codec (`transforms.py`):**
- `SessionDict` — a **value-keyed** intern table. Value-keying is the crux that makes one shared instance safe across peers (a value is a value regardless of emitter), unlike the tool-keyed diff base whose correctness depends on per-peer isolation. Bounded + LRU like the drop store; its lock is a **leaf** (never held with an Interceptor lock → no lock-ordering entanglement).
- `sess_encode` — define-once, reference-thereafter. A value already interned emits as a bare alias with its **definition elided** (the cross-peer win, paid once, possibly by another peer). A new value that repeats in-payload *or* is a lone high-token **string** is interned + defined first-use; a lone unique **subtree** is never speculatively interned (it never recurs and would swallow its shared leaves).
- Guards: bails on a payload literal colliding with any live alias (`#6`, cumulative-legend safety), bails on a pre-existing terse marker, and **self-verifies the round-trip against the client's full cumulative legend or returns `None` to fall back** — the hard lossless guarantee.
- `sess_decode` reuses `dict_decode` (alias resolution is identical; the legend is just session-cumulative).

**Wiring seam (`proxy.py`, `multiproxy.py`):**
- `Interceptor` gains an optional `session_dict`, mirroring the shared `store`/`store_lock` idiom (`None` = off = behavior-preserving for single-peer callers).
- `run_multi_proxy` mints one `SessionDict` and injects it into every peer's Interceptor via `_build_peers` — the same seam the shared drop store already travels.

## Safety model (why this is the right shape)

A session reference can point at a definition many payloads back — a structurally higher dangling-reference rate than the `--diff` tier's one-message-back reference. So beyond the `--diff` playbook (opt-in, keyframe, reconnect-clear, self-verify), stage 3 will **back references with the existing drop-to-retrieve store**: a compacted-out definition becomes recoverable via `terse.retrieve` instead of silently corrupt. Stages 1-2 are structured so that slots in without redesign.

## Verification

13 new tests — cross-peer elision proven (payload B references A's values with **zero new defs**), round-trip, LRU/clear, `#6` collision bail, marker passthrough, wiring seam. **438 pass.** Confirmed inert: `sess_encode` has no call site in the compress flow.

## Remaining (stages 3-4, not in this PR)

3. Guards — keyframe re-emission bound, reconnect-clear, primer extension, retrieve-backed references.
4. Opt-in `--session-dict` flag (default off, like `--diff`) + wire into `measure`; validate the realized saving on the co-resident corpus.

title: Add terse probe --cross-server: #64 Phase 0 cross-peer redundancy gate
body:
## What

Implements the **#64 Phase 0 probe** — the measurement gate that decides whether a shared cross-peer dictionary (Phase 1, Effort L) is worth building, or whether the gateway stays pure ergonomics. Measurement only; compresses nothing.

`terse probe --cross-server [--cap N]` — groups the capture corpus by origin server and measures cross-server redundancy two ways:

- **Lever A — value dictionary:** pooled-vs-per-peer `est_dict_saving` increment over extracted record cells. Isolates values repeated across ≥2 *distinct* servers (a within-peer repeat is already folded per-peer).
- **Lever B — framing-normalized overlap:** idf-weighted cross-server raw-token overlap. Weighting by inverse document frequency drops ubiquitous JSON-framing tokens (`{`, `"`, `:`, common keys), so only shared **content** counts. Shape-agnostic — it spans text/source servers (codegraph) that Lever A can't see.

Server identity is reverse-mapped from the captured tool name (the capture envelope stores only `tool`; the corpus was captured by separate per-server proxies sharing one `--capture-dir`).

## Coverage guard (why the verdict is honest)

Lever A only sees record-shaped payloads. When a server present in the corpus produces none (text/source or pre-compressed output), the report flags Lever A **blind** for that server and the verdict falls back to Lever B's framing-netted content overlap — rather than emitting a false "close" on data it couldn't see. The probe gates on **raw** payloads (≥2 servers), so Lever B always runs even on an all-text corpus.

## Result — cross-session corpus (770 payloads, kb + codegraph + runecho)

- Lever A **blind to codegraph** — 0 record-shaped payloads (its output is source/text); only 7 of 770 payloads are record-shaped.
- Lever B: raw overlap median **14.8% → content 8.2%** after framing normalization (≈45% of the apparent overlap was framing).
- **Verdict on this corpus: INCONCLUSIVE.** The corpus is cross-session and about *different* entities per server, so 8.2% is a **floor**, not a real measurement of co-residency.

## Result — co-resident capture (same terse symbols across all three servers)

Drove kb + codegraph + runecho about the **same** terse symbols/files in one batch (35 payloads), then re-ran the probe:

- Framing-netted content overlap **20.9%** (raw 45.2%) — a **2.5× jump** over the cross-session floor.
- **codegraph is the hub:** up to **54%** content overlap with runecho (shared symbol structure), 20–36% with kb (shared identifiers/decisions). kb×runecho is near-zero (prose vs symbols).
- **Verdict: LEAN BUILD.** Substantial shared content a per-peer coder misses; a shared cross-peer legend has real headroom. Phase 1's dictionary would pay mainly on the codegraph↔{runecho, kb} edges.

## Next step

Design Phase 1 (shared session dictionary across peers), verifying the 20.9% pattern holds on an independent co-resident capture. Inherits #37's tensions (cross-stream diff-desync #20, keyframe cadence #8, marker-collision #6 now spanning N peers).

## Tests

8 new probe tests (idf weighting, framing netting, cross-server levers, empty-Lever-A degradation, coverage). Full suite **425 passing**.

title: Gateway v2: cross-server session dictionary (the compression native multiplex can't do) + close v1 fan-out gaps
body:
**Value 2 · Effort L · probe-gated**

## What already exists (not this issue)

The multi-downstream gateway *plumbing* landed with #5: `multiproxy.py` runs one
`terse proxy --config` process fronting N peers (stdio + HTTP), merges `tools/list`
under name prefixes, routes `tools/call` by prefix, and shares the drop-to-retrieve
store across peers. That part is done.

Its own docstring is honest about the ceiling: *"explicitly an ergonomics feature —
MCP clients already multiplex servers natively — so the v1 scope is deliberately
proportionate."* One process / one policy / one primer is real but modest value,
because the client can already point at N servers itself.

## The actual gap: the compression a client's native multiplex *can't* do

The one thing a terse gateway can do that the client's own multiplexing cannot is
**cross-server compression** — a shared, session-level dictionary/legend that intern
strings and repeated subtrees *across peers*, not just within one tool's output.
Today peers share only the drop store; each `Interceptor` compresses in isolation
(confirmed: no interning/session-dict in `multiproxy.py` or `proxy.py`). #37
(session-level cross-call dictionary) was closed as deferred — "gated on a redundancy
probe + the diff-desync tension." The gateway is exactly the context that could make
it pay off, *if* the redundancy is there.

### Phase 0 — probe first (gate the build on evidence)

Consistent with terse's measurement-first ethos (per-tool 0–30% wins were *measured*,
not assumed): before building anything, measure cross-server redundancy. Do outputs
from real co-resident servers (runecho + kb + codegraph, captured via `--capture-dir`)
actually share enough strings/subtrees to beat per-peer compression? Reuse the
existing `probe` value-redundancy machinery, scoped across peers.
**If cross-server overlap is negligible, close this — the gateway stays pure ergonomics.**

### Phase 1 — shared session dictionary across peers (only if Phase 0 clears)

- A single session legend spanning all peers, injected once, referenced by every
  peer's compressed output.
- Inherits #37's unresolved tensions: cross-call **diff-desync** (guard from #20) and
  **keyframe** cadence (#8) now span multiple independent streams — a shared legend
  is a shared dangling-reference surface. Marker-collision guard (#6) must hold across
  peers, not just within one.

### Phase 2 (independent, small) — close the documented v1 fan-out gaps

`multiproxy.py` documents these deliberately: `resources/list`, `prompts/list`, `ping`
(any non-`tools/*` client method) are forwarded to **peer 0 only**, silently dropping
N-1 peers' data. Broadcast+merge these like `tools/list` already is. Standalone from
the compression work; ships value even if Phase 0 kills Phases 1.

## Prior art (researched 2026-07)

**MCP gateways — none compress output.** A Q1-2026 ecosystem survey of ~17 aggregation
gateways frames the gap as governance/RBAC, not context reduction
([survey](https://www.heyitworks.tech/blog/mcp-aggregation-gateway-proxy-tools-q1-2026)).
Verified individually — all routing/namespacing/transport only, zero output compression:
TBXark/mcp-proxy, [MetaMCP](https://github.com/metatool-ai/metamcp),
[mcgravity](https://github.com/tigranbs/mcgravity),
[IBM ContextForge](https://github.com/IBM/mcp-context-forge),
[Docker MCP Gateway](https://docs.docker.com/ai/mcp-catalog-and-toolkit/mcp-gateway/) ("doesn't
optimize tool responses"), [Lasso mcp-gateway](https://github.com/lasso-security/mcp-gateway),
[pluggedin](https://github.com/VeriTeknik/pluggedin-mcp-proxy),
[sparfenyuk/mcp-proxy](https://github.com/sparfenyuk/mcp-proxy) (bridge only).
So the *gateway + compression* combination is unclaimed.

**Adjacent compression work.**
- [TOON](https://openapi.com/blog/what-the-toon-format-is-token-oriented-object-notation) —
  lossless tabular JSON re-encoding (30–60%); same family as terse's tabularize tier.
- [Atlassian mcp-compressor](https://www.atlassian.com/blog/development/mcp-compression-preventing-tool-bloat-in-ai-agents) —
  lossless but compresses **tool definitions only** (cf. our #35), not call results, no cross-call dict.
- [LLMLingua-2](https://github.com/microsoft/LLMLingua) — lossy extractive prompt compression, not MCP-shaped.
- Anthropic [code-execution-with-MCP](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/code-execution-with-mcp)
  and [tool-search / defer_loading](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use) —
  cut **definition/orchestration** tokens, orthogonal to result-payload compression.

**Nearest neighbor — [Headroom](https://github.com/chopratejas/headroom).** Already does
cross-call/cross-agent output dedup via a shared store — so "nobody does cross-call
dictionaries" is **false**, cite it honestly. Differentiators: Headroom is lossy-first
(retrieval-on-demand for originals) vs terse's lossless-first with no decode step; and
it's a compression layer bolted onto passing MCP traffic, **not** a gateway that
namespaces tools across N stdio-spawned server identities. UNVERIFIED whether its dedup
store keys on distinct MCP server identity the way our prefix-per-peer routing does —
worth confirming before claiming that specific differentiation.

## Novelty (net)

Genuinely unclaimed: a stdio gateway that is *both* an N-server multiplexer with prefix
namespacing *and* applies a **lossless-first, session-level cross-server** string/subtree
dictionary to tool **results**. But value is real only if Phase 0 shows cross-server
redundancy exists — hence probe-gated, not build-first.

Refs: #5 (gateway plumbing, done), #37 (session dict, deferred), #35 (tool-def compression),
#20 (diff-desync guard), #8 (keyframes), #6 (marker-collision guard), #9 (diff-note overhead).

title: Add terse fluency --text-diff-eval: behavioral gate for text-diff codec
body:
## Summary
- `text_diff.py` (Tier 0.7, non-JSON tool output) had a round-trip correctness gate but no proof a model can actually use the diff form — unlike the JSON diff, which has `fluency --diff`.
- Adds `gen_text_diff_questions`/`run_text_diff_payload`/`run_text_diff_fluency` in `fluency.py`, reusing `run_diff_payload`'s paired-comparison design; reuses `diff_gap_rows`/`build_terminal_diff_report` unchanged.
- `build_diff_report` split into a shared `_build_diff_style_report` helper (zero behavior change, pinned by a regression test) so the new `build_text_diff_report` can correctly label its control column "raw text" instead of "full-terse" (Tier 0 doesn't compress non-JSON text).
- New `--text-diff-eval` flag on `terse fluency`, wired the same way as `--diff`.
- `TECHNICAL.md`/`USAGE.md` updated: closes the "Known Limitations" gap this exact plan was written to close.

## Test plan
- [x] `uv run pytest -q` — 413 passed (7 new tests in `tests/test_fluency.py`)
- [x] `uv run ruff check` — clean
- [x] Live end-to-end trace: captured a real two-call text corpus via `terse capture`, ran the actual `load_corpus` → `run_text_diff_fluency` → `build_text_diff_report`/terminal-bars path with a scripted oracle answerer — confirms harness plumbing (no live model configured in this session, so this proves plumbing correctness, not model comprehension — same caveat `--diff`/`--drop-eval` carry).
